mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers Keynesian effective demand theory, unemployment theory, monetary policy, and countercyclical fiscal policy (A.1, C.3).
Bridges micro and macro analysis, explaining how inflation, investment, and unemployment connect individual decisions to aggregate outcomes (A).
National income, GDP, and aggregate output are the building blocks of macroeconomic analysis introduced here (A.1, A.2).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John O. S. Wilson, John Goddard
mappings.rationaleAI: Central banking, monetary policy transmission, financial crises, and sovereign debt have profound macroeconomic effects (B.1, B.2, C.3).
International monetary institutions, balance of payments mechanics, and the Bretton Woods system shaped the postwar macroeconomic order (B.2).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Depression represents the defining business cycle collapse (C.1), and the New Deal demonstrated countercyclical fiscal and monetary policy in action (C.3).
Interest rates, arbitrage, and financial instruments are relevant to foreign exchange mechanics and monetary analysis (B.1.a).
Business cycles, financial crises, and boom-bust dynamics are inherent features of capitalist macroeconomies (C).
International economic integration, cross-border capital flows, and balance of payments challenges are macroeconomic phenomena intensified by globalization (B).
Smith's classical law of markets laid the intellectual foundation from which macroeconomic thought later diverged (A.1.a).
Historical business cycles, financial panics, and their macroeconomic consequences illustrate recurring patterns across centuries (C.1).
Monetarist and supply-side approaches emerged as alternatives to Keynesian demand management, reshaping fiscal and monetary policy debates (C.3).
Developing countries face distinctive macroeconomic challenges—balance of payments deficits, exchange rate instability, capital flight—examined here (B).
Taxation is a primary fiscal policy tool, and its design as a countercyclical instrument has significant macroeconomic effects (C.3).
Marx theorized that capitalist crises and business cycles are systemic rather than accidental, offering an alternative lens on macroeconomic instability (C.2).
